_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for St. Martin Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the historic heart of Acadiana. St. Martinville -- the parish seat and the legendary 'Evangeline' landing site -- was the cultural and administrative capital of the Acadian exile community from the 1760s. The Attakapas Post records at St. Martinville are among the oldest and most genealogically significant documents in southwestern Louisiana.
display_name: St. Martin Parish, Louisiana -- Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: St. Martinville, LA -- 415 South Main Street. Modern courthouse in downtown St. Martinville, near the Bayou Teche.

previous_courthouses.location: St. Martinville -- early frame courthouse near the Bayou Teche landing. St. Martin was one of the original parishes created in 1807.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a more substantial building.

previous_courthouses.location: St. Martinville -- brick courthouse on Main Street, an antebellum-era structure that served for over a century
previous_courthouses.fate: Survived the Civil War, multiple hurricanes, and heavy use. Replaced by the current modern courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Daughter parish creation -- Lafayette (1823) and Iberia (1868) carved from St. Martin
records_survived: Pre-creation records for Lafayette and Iberia areas remain at St. Martin
record_losses.description: St. Martin Parish was the mother parish for much of central Acadiana. Lafayette Parish was carved from it in 1823, and Iberia Parish was carved from it (and St. Mary) in 1868. All records predating these creation dates for the Lafayette and New Iberia areas remain at St. Martinville. This makes the St. Martin Parish courthouse one of the most important genealogical repositories in Louisiana -- it holds the Attakapas colonial records that are the foundation of Acadian family history in the region.
record_losses.narrative_impact: St. Martin is the mother of central Acadiana parishes. If {ancestor_name} settled anywhere in the Attakapas district -- whether their descendants later lived in Lafayette, Iberia, or St. Martin Parish -- the earliest family records are at St. Martinville.
record_losses.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.

record_losses.cause: Civil War -- Banks's Red River Campaign and Teche operations
records_destroyed: Scattered loose papers; some Confederate-era militia and administrative records
record_losses.description: Federal forces moved through the Bayou Teche corridor in 1863 during the Teche Campaign and the Red River Campaign. St. Martinville was occupied but the courthouse was not targeted for destruction. Some loose papers were lost during the occupation. The colonial-era bound records and the critical conveyance and succession books survived intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Federal occupation disrupted civil government but did not destroy the records that make St. Martinville's courthouse the essential repository for Acadian genealogy. The colonial records survived because they were bound volumes, not loose papers.
record_losses.source: Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.

deeds.completeness: Excellent -- conveyance records from the colonial Attakapas Post era survive, including French and Spanish land grants. The series is continuous from the colonial period through the present, making St. Martin one of the richest courthouses in Louisiana for early records.
deeds.gaps: Some colonial-era loose papers are fragmentary. The bound conveyance books are intact. The earliest documents are in French and Spanish.
deeds.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville
deeds.online_access: Limited -- some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records from the colonial period survive. The antebellum succession inventories for the sugar plantations along Bayou Teche are extraordinarily detailed.
wills_estates.gaps: Some colonial-era successions are fragmentary. The bulk of the collection is intact from the early 1800s forward.
wills_estates.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville

marriages.completeness: Good from early statehood. Catholic sacramental records at St. Martin de Tours Church (established 1765) are significantly earlier and are the primary marriage source for the colonial and early American period.
marriages.gaps: Civil marriage records begin at statehood. Church records fill the colonial gap entirely.
marriages.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.completeness: Good -- continuous from early statehood. Colonial-era administrative records from the Attakapas commandant supplement court records.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor disruption during Federal occupation 1863. Colonial commandant records are at the Louisiana State Archives.
court_minutes.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- compliance was slow in rural areas. Church records are significantly more reliable and earlier.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.source_type: St. Martin de Tours Church sacramental records
substitute_sources.description: St. Martin de Tours Church in St. Martinville (established 1765) is one of the oldest Catholic churches in Acadiana. Its sacramental registers document baptisms, marriages, and burials from the founding of the Acadian community at the Attakapas Post. These are the foundational documents of Acadian genealogy in Louisiana.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Martin de Tours Church, St. Martinville; Diocese of Lafayette archives; abstracted in Hebert
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The single most important source for tracing Acadian families from their arrival in Louisiana. The early registers are in French and Latin and document the exiles' first marriages, baptisms, and deaths in Louisiana. Godparent relationships reveal the kinship networks that held the exile community together.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: The Attakapas Post -- the Spanish colonial administrative center at St. Martinville -- generated records including censuses, land grants, commandant correspondence, and judicial proceedings. Some remain at the St. Martin courthouse; others are at the Louisiana State Archives and in Spanish colonial archives.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court; Louisiana State Archives; Archivo General de Indias (microfilm)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Colonial censuses are especially valuable -- they list household heads with wives, children, and enslaved people by name and age. The 1766, 1771, 1777, and 1785 Attakapas censuses are among the most important documents in Acadian genealogy.
substitute_sources.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.

substitute_sources.description: The notarial archives at St. Martinville include colonial-era acts, marriage contracts, emancipation papers, and property transactions executed before notaries public under the civil law system. These records survive in better condition than at many Louisiana courthouses.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Marriage contracts (contrats de mariage) are especially valuable -- they detail the property each spouse brought to the marriage and name family members on both sides. Emancipation acts document the freeing of enslaved people and sometimes reveal family relationships.
substitute_sources.source: Conrad, Glenn R. The French Experience in Louisiana. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1995.

narrative_hooks: St. Martinville was where the exiles gathered -- the Attakapas Post church where {ancestor_name}'s family was baptized, married, and buried is still standing, and its registers from 1765 are the birth certificate of Acadian Louisiana.

The colonial censuses at St. Martinville name every member of {ancestor_name}'s household -- wife, children, enslaved people, and even livestock -- a snapshot of exile life as detailed as any in the colonial South.

St. Martin is the mother parish of central Acadiana -- if {ancestor_name}'s family later lived in Lafayette or Iberia Parish, their earliest records are at St. Martinville, where the Attakapas Post commandant wrote in French and the priest baptized in Latin.

The marriage contracts in St. Martin's notarial archives list what each spouse brought to the marriage -- every cow, every plow, every arpent of land -- under a Napoleonic Code system that treated {ancestor_name}'s marriage as a business partnership with legal documents to match.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia: The Beginnings of Acadian Life in Louisiana, 1765-1803. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
Conrad, Glenn R. The French Experience in Louisiana. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1995.
Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.
Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun: Transformation of a People, 1803-1877. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
